title: Abdominal pain, vomiting, and rapid breathing in a young diabetic on new agents
diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis presenting as abdominal pain

chart.oneLiner: 24F with type 1 diabetes — recently started on an SGLT2 inhibitor and a thiazide, on levothyroxine, taking over-the-counter aspirin for a sprained ankle — presents with one day of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and rapid breathing after a flu-like illness during which she ate poorly.

opening: My stomach has been killing me and I can't stop throwing up, and I feel like I just can't catch my breath.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes, looks unwell and dehydrated, breathing fast and deep. Tired and a bit anxious but cooperative; answers slowly and is a little drowsy.

HPI: ~24h of diffuse, crampy abdominal pain with persistent nausea and frequent vomiting. Over the same period she has felt very thirsty, has been urinating large amounts, and feels increasingly weak and breathless even at rest. A friend said her breath smells fruity. She had a flu-like illness (fever, sore throat, poor appetite, ~3 days of barely eating) over the past 3-4 days. NO hematemesis, NO black or bloody stools, NO localized RLQ pain or pain with movement, NO dysuria, NO vaginal discharge, NO chest pain, NO tinnitus or ringing in the ears (ask-only). Last menstrual period was normal/on time (no chance of pregnancy by her account).
PMH: Type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 14 on a basal-bolus insulin regimen; one prior similar hospitalization a few years ago when she was sick and stopped insulin (if asked). Hashimoto hypothyroidism. Newly diagnosed mild hypertension. Sprained her right ankle ~5 days ago (if asked). No prior abdominal surgery, no gallstones, no pancreatitis.
MEDS (volunteer the new ones / the aspirin / the SGLT2 only when asked specifically about medications, new prescriptions, over-the-counter drugs, or what she has been taking): insulin glargine at night and insulin aspart with meals. Because she felt sick and was not eating she SKIPPED some mealtime insulin and cut back her basal over the last couple of days (only if asked about insulin adherence/doses or recent illness). About 3 weeks ago her endocrinologist ADDED empagliflozin (an SGLT2 inhibitor) to help her numbers, and her PCP started hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure. She has been taking fairly large amounts of over-the-counter aspirin (several tablets several times a day) for the ankle for ~5 days. Also on levothyroxine. No new antibiotics. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: college student; rare alcohol (a single glass of wine occasionally, none in the last week — ask-only); non-smoker; no recreational drugs; not sexually active currently. FHx: father type 2 diabetes; mother Hashimoto thyroiditis.
ROS: as in HPI; no focal neuro symptoms; no tinnitus; no fevers now; no flank pain.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, fatigued, mildly drowsy, breathing deeply and rapidly (Kussmaul respirations); fruity/acetone odor on the breath.
- Vitals: BP 100/62, HR 124, RR 30, 37.7C, SpO2 99% RA.
- HEENT/skin: dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor (volume depletion). No scleral icterus.
- Abdomen: mild, diffuse tenderness on deep palpation WITHOUT rebound, guarding, or rigidity; soft, bowel sounds present; no epigastric mass, no flank ecchymosis; no localized RLQ/RUQ tenderness; negative Murphy and psoas signs.
- Neuro: alert and oriented x3, mildly drowsy/fatigued but no focal deficit; no asterixis.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the skipped insulin, the polyuria/polydipsia, the new empagliflozin/HCTZ, or the heavy aspirin use unless asked about insulin adherence, diabetes control, urination, thirst, the recent illness, new prescriptions, or over-the-counter/medication use.
- Do NOT volunteer the fruity breath; if the examiner comments on or examines the breath/general appearance, confirm it.
- If asked about alcohol, say you rarely drink and have had none in the last week.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer in lay language.

labs.BMP: Glucose 540, anion gap WIDE at 26 (Na 130, Cl 95, HCO3 9). Corrected Na ~141 (add ~2.4 mEq per 100 mg/dL glucose over 100, i.e. 130 + 2.4 x 4.4 = 140.6) — the measured Na 130 is pseudo-hyponatremia from hyperglycemia (and hypertriglyceridemia), NOT true hyponatremia. K 5.6 (total body potassium DEPLETED despite this value, from osmotic/GI losses and acidosis-driven shift). BUN 36, Cr 1.6 (prerenal AKI on a normal baseline).
labs.VBG: pH 7.21, HCO3 9, pCO2 23 — a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory (Kussmaul) compensation (Winter's expected pCO2 = 1.5 x 9 + 8 = ~21-22, measured 23 → no additional respiratory disorder; the heavy aspirin has NOT produced a concurrent respiratory alkalosis).
labs.Ketones: Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate markedly ELEVATED (>5 mmol/L); urine ketones large/3+ — confirms ketosis. Note: bedside nitroprusside dipsticks detect acetoacetate and can UNDER-read beta-hydroxybutyrate-predominant DKA, so a serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is preferred.
labs.Lipase_TG: Lipase mildly elevated at ~1.5x the upper limit of normal (can be nonspecifically raised in DKA and does NOT by itself diagnose pancreatitis); triglycerides elevated at ~620 mg/dL (contributes to pseudo-hyponatremia and is a recognized DKA-associated derangement). No imaging evidence of pancreatitis.
labs.CBC: WBC 16.2 with neutrophil predominance (stress demargination/ketosis — can be elevated WITHOUT infection), Hgb/Hct mildly elevated from hemoconcentration, Plt normal.
labs.Salicylate_Lactate: Serum salicylate level LOW/sub-therapeutic (~6 mg/dL) — excludes salicylate toxicity as the cause of the wide gap despite the aspirin history. Lactate mildly elevated at 2.4 (hypoperfusion/ketosis), not the dominant gap contributor.
labs.UA_hCG: Urinalysis with large ketones and heavy glucosuria, no nitrites/leukocyte esterase (no UTI); urine beta-hCG NEGATIVE (excludes pregnancy).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, narrow-based, peaked symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (serum K 5.6); no acute ischemic ST changes, intervals otherwise preserved.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with peaked T waves of hyperkalemia. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia and a still-narrow QRS
ekg.clinicalVignette: A young diabetic with nausea, abdominal pain, rapid deep breathing, and a serum potassium of 5.6.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lungs with no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm; normal cardiac silhouette.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC file showing a normal PA chest film; must NOT show free air, consolidation, or effusion.
cxr.imageDescription: normal chest radiograph, clear lungs, no free air
cxr.clinicalVignette: A young woman with diabetes and abdominal pain has a chest film to exclude an infectious precipitant and free air.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this young diabetic woman's abdominal pain (note your leading diagnosis). She was recently started on an SGLT2 inhibitor and a thiazide, takes levothyroxine, and has been using a lot of over-the-counter aspirin. Also NAME THREE distinct mechanisms/etiologies that could produce her high anion gap acidosis, and say which you favor.
steps.rubric: Diabetic ketoacidosis (leading,7). Acute pancreatitis as a competing driver of the pain (and possible DKA trigger, given the mildly elevated lipase and hypertriglyceridemia) — CANT-MISS competitor (CANT-MISS,4). Surgical abdomen — appendicitis/perforation (CANT-MISS,3). Salicylate toxicity from heavy aspirin use as an alternative high-gap acidosis (CANT-MISS,2). Names THREE mechanisms of a high anion gap acidosis — e.g. ketoacidosis (diabetic/starvation/alcoholic), lactic acidosis/sepsis, toxic ingestion (salicylate, methanol/ethylene glycol), uremia (core,3). Recognizes SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic DKA as a relevant mechanism/precipitant given the new empagliflozin (core,2). Ectopic pregnancy must be excluded in a woman of childbearing age (core,2). Gastroenteritis / starvation ketosis from poor intake during illness (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on a surgical abdomen and rushing to CT before checking a gap/ketones (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis — a type 1 diabetic who under-dosed insulin during a febrile illness, now with Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath, polyuria/polydipsia, and a benign but diffusely tender abdomen (DKA itself causes abdominal pain). The genuinely competing diagnosis is acute pancreatitis as an alternative/additional driver of the pain (and a recognized DKA precipitant) — kept live by the mildly elevated lipase and hypertriglyceridemia, and resolved only by the labs/imaging. Must-not-miss alternatives: a surgical abdomen (appendicitis/perforation), and salicylate toxicity from heavy OTC aspirin as a different high-gap acidosis. THREE mechanisms of a high anion gap acidosis to name: (1) ketoacidosis — diabetic, starvation (poor intake for days), or alcoholic; (2) lactic acidosis from sepsis/hypoperfusion; (3) a toxic ingestion — classically salicylate or methanol/ethylene glycol — plus uremia. I favor ketoacidosis. Also note SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic DKA as a precipitant given her new empagliflozin, exclude ectopic pregnancy with a beta-hCG, and consider gastroenteritis/starvation ketosis.

criticalActions.item: Acute pancreatitis as a competing driver/precipitant (mild lipase + hypertriglyceridemia)
Salicylate toxicity from heavy aspirin (alternative high-gap acidosis)

coreActions.item: Names THREE mechanisms of a high anion gap acidosis (ketoacidosis / lactic / toxic ingestion / uremia)
Recognizes SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic DKA as a precipitant (new empagliflozin)
Gastroenteritis / starvation ketosis from poor intake during illness

penalties.item: Anchors on a surgical abdomen and rushes to CT before checking the gap/ketones

mustNotMiss: Acute pancreatitis as a competing driver/precipitant

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick/serum glucose + BMP for anion gap and potassium (CANT-MISS,4). SERUM beta-hydroxybutyrate (preferred over urine dipstick, which under-reads beta-hydroxybutyrate) (CANT-MISS,4). Venous blood gas for pH (3). Targeted disambiguating labs — lipase + triglycerides (pancreatitis fork), salicylate level + lactate (alternative gap), urine beta-hCG (2). CBC, UA, EKG for hyperkalemia, CXR for an infectious trigger/free air (1). PENALTY: rushing to insulin before checking potassium and starting fluids (-2); ordering an early abdominal CT for the pain before checking the gap/ketones (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; BMP (glucose, gap, potassium, renal function); VBG; SERUM beta-hydroxybutyrate (not just a urine ketone dipstick); lipase and triglycerides to address the pancreatitis fork; a salicylate level and lactate to address the alternative high-gap acidosis; CBC; UA; urine beta-hCG to exclude pregnancy.

criticalActions.item: Glucose + BMP (anion gap, potassium, renal function)

penalties.item: Rushing to insulin before checking potassium and starting fluids
Ordering an early abdominal CT for the pain before checking the gap/ketones

requiredTests: Glucose + BMP (anion gap, potassium, renal function)
contraindicatedTests: Ordering an early abdominal CT for the pain before checking the gap/ketones
orderSensitiveActions: Rushing to insulin before checking potassium and starting fluids

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they change your immediate management.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, narrow-based symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (serum K 5.6); no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (4); recognizes peaked T waves / hyperkalemia pattern (6); ties the EKG hyperkalemia to the DKA picture — serum K is high but TOTAL-BODY potassium is depleted and will FALL once insulin and fluids start, so K must be monitored and repleted and insulin held if K < 3.3 (3); systematic read, no acute ischemic ST changes (2). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing peaked T waves (-3).
keyFindings: tall peaked symmetric T waves reflecting hyperkalemia
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.6); no acute ischemia.
Ties the EKG hyperkalemia to a total-body potassium deficit that will fall with insulin/fluids — monitor and replete K, hold insulin if K < 3.3.
commonMistakes: dismissing peaked T waves as a normal variant
not linking the EKG hyperkalemia to the total-body potassium deficit in DKA
starting insulin without using the EKG/K to guide potassium management
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia (rate ~120) with tall, peaked, narrow-based, symmetric T waves consistent with hyperkalemia (serum K 5.6); QRS not yet widened and no P-wave loss, and no acute ischemic ST changes. This is the patient's own tracing and it directly changes management: the serum potassium is high but TOTAL-BODY potassium is depleted in DKA and will drop sharply once insulin and fluids are given. So I will put her on a monitor, recheck potassium frequently, add potassium to fluids once K is < 5.2, and hold insulin if K is < 3.3 until it is repleted.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.6); no acute ischemia.
Ties the EKG hyperkalemia to a total-body potassium deficit that will fall with insulin/fluids — monitor and replete K, hold insulin if K < 3.3.
coreActions: tall peaked symmetric T waves reflecting hyperkalemia

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the peaked T waves

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear film, no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film with clear lungs (5); explicitly excludes a precipitant/mimic — pneumonia (infectious trigger) and free air (perforation) (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
criticalFindings: Clear lungs; no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm.
commonMistakes: forgetting the CXR helps screen for an infectious DKA trigger
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lungs with no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm; normal cardiac silhouette. Useful to exclude pneumonia as a DKA precipitant and free air from perforation.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: clear lungs; no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm.

coreActions.item: excludes pneumonia (infectious trigger) and free air (perforation)

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Results: glucose 540, anion gap 26 (Na 130, Cl 95, HCO3 9; corrected Na ~141), VBG pH 7.21 with HCO3 9 and pCO2 23, serum beta-hydroxybutyrate markedly elevated with 3+ urine ketones, lipase ~1.5x ULN with triglycerides 620, salicylate level sub-therapeutic, lactate 2.4, beta-hCG negative, and an EKG with peaked T waves. Commit to a diagnosis, state the single discriminating finding for EACH competitor you ruled out, and name any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Diabetic ketoacidosis (6). Cites the defining triad — hyperglycemia, high anion gap metabolic acidosis (gap 26, pH 7.21, low bicarbonate with appropriate Winter's compensation), and ketonemia/ketonuria with elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate — and explains the abdominal pain is from the DKA itself. Resolves the fork with discriminators: pancreatitis ruled out as the primary process because the lipase is only mildly raised (nonspecific in DKA) with no imaging pancreatitis (the hypertriglyceridemia is DKA-associated); salicylate toxicity excluded by the sub-therapeutic level; surgical abdomen excluded by the benign exam; pregnancy excluded by negative hCG; the measured Na 130 is pseudo-hyponatremia (corrected ~141, so no true hyponatremia to chase) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Diabetic ketoacidosis — the defining triad of hyperglycemia (glucose 540), a high anion gap metabolic acidosis (gap 26 from Na 130 − Cl 95 − HCO3 9; pH 7.21 with appropriate Winter's respiratory compensation), and ketosis (markedly elevated serum beta-hydroxybutyrate, 3+ urine ketones). The diffuse abdominal pain is from the DKA itself and should resolve as the acidosis clears. Discriminators for each competitor: acute pancreatitis is NOT the primary process — the lipase is only ~1.5x ULN, which is nonspecific in DKA, with no imaging pancreatitis, and the triglycerides of 620 are a DKA-associated derangement rather than proof of pancreatitis; salicylate toxicity is excluded by the sub-therapeutic salicylate level despite the aspirin history; a surgical abdomen is excluded by the benign, non-peritonitic exam; ectopic pregnancy is excluded by the negative beta-hCG. The measured Na of 130 is pseudo-hyponatremia from hyperglycemia and hypertriglyceridemia (corrected Na ~141), so there is no true hyponatremia to treat. Residual uncertainty: the SGLT2 inhibitor can blunt the glucose and produce euglycemic DKA, so the gap/ketones — not the glucose — drive monitoring; I would keep a low threshold to re-image if the abdominal pain fails to track with the closing gap.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and what must change before she is safely discharged?
steps.rubric: Per MGH DKA protocol: aggressive isotonic IV fluids — normal saline first (CANT-MISS,5); check and REPLETE potassium before/with insulin, hold insulin if K < 3.3 and use the EKG peaked-T-wave finding to guide this (CANT-MISS,5); IV regular insulin infusion after fluids/K addressed (4); add dextrose when glucose ~200 and continue insulin until the gap closes — track the GAP/ketones not the glucose (especially with the SGLT2 inhibitor on board) (3); STOP the SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) and hold the thiazide; identify/treat the precipitant — under-dosed insulin during illness +/- SGLT2-precipitated euglycemic DKA (3); frequent monitoring of glucose, gap, and electrolytes (2). SAFE DISCHARGE/DISPOSITION: admit (step-down/ICU if severe acidosis or drowsiness); do not discharge until the gap has closed and she is eating, transition to subcutaneous insulin with 1-2h overlap, permanently discontinue the SGLT2 inhibitor, give sick-day rules (never fully stop basal insulin, check ketones when ill) and diabetes education, and arrange close endocrine follow-up (3). PENALTY: giving insulin before checking/repleting potassium (-3); bicarbonate for this mild-moderate acidosis (-2); stopping the insulin drip on glucose normalization before the gap closes (-2); continuing the SGLT2 inhibitor (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate with isotonic IV fluids (normal saline) first. CHECK potassium before insulin and use the EKG (peaked T waves, K 5.6) to guide it: total-body K is depleted and will fall fast — if K < 3.3 hold insulin and replete; if K 3.3-5.2 give insulin with K added to fluids; if K > 5.2 start insulin and recheck. Start an IV regular insulin infusion once fluids/K are addressed; add dextrose-containing fluids when glucose reaches ~200 and continue the insulin until the ANION GAP normalizes — follow the gap/ketones, not the glucose, which is especially important because her SGLT2 inhibitor can keep glucose deceptively low (euglycemic DKA). STOP the empagliflozin and hold the hydrochlorothiazide. Treat the precipitant: under-dosed insulin during a viral illness, with the SGLT2 inhibitor as a contributor. Monitor glucose hourly and the gap/electrolytes q2-4h. Avoid bicarbonate at this pH. DISPOSITION: admit (step-down or ICU given drowsiness and the acidosis). SAFE DISCHARGE: do not discharge until the gap has closed and she is eating; transition to subcutaneous insulin with a 1-2 hour overlap with the drip; PERMANENTLY discontinue the SGLT2 inhibitor; teach sick-day rules (never stop basal insulin entirely, check ketones when ill, stay hydrated), counsel that heavy OTC aspirin can confuse the picture, and arrange close endocrinology follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Check and replete potassium before/with insulin, EKG-guided (hold insulin if K < 3.3)

coreActions.item: IV regular insulin infusion after fluids/K addressed
Add dextrose at glucose ~200 and continue insulin until the gap closes (track gap, not glucose)
Stop the SGLT2 inhibitor and treat the precipitant (under-dosed insulin during illness)
Frequent monitoring of glucose, gap, and electrolytes
Safe discharge: gap closed and eating, transition to SC insulin with overlap, permanently stop SGLT2i, sick-day rules/education, close endocrine follow-up

penalties.item: Stopping the insulin drip on glucose normalization before the gap closes

requiredActions: Check and replete potassium before/with insulin, EKG-guided (hold insulin if K < 3.3)
optionalActions: IV regular insulin infusion after fluids/K addressed
Add dextrose at glucose ~200 and continue insulin until the gap closes (track gap, not glucose)
Stop the SGLT2 inhibitor and treat the precipitant (under-dosed insulin during illness)
Frequent monitoring of glucose, gap, and electrolytes
Safe discharge: gap closed and eating, transition to SC insulin with overlap, permanently stop SGLT2i, sick-day rules/education, close endocrine follow-up
unsafeActions: Stopping the insulin drip on glucose normalization before the gap closes
steps.disposition: Admit (step-down/ICU if severe acidosis or altered mental status)

Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: ill-appearing, fatigued, mildly drowsy, breathing deeply and rapidly (Kussmaul respirations); fruity/acetone odor on the breath.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Comfortable, breathing normally, no unusual breath odor.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin: poor skin turgor consistent with dehydration.
Abdomen: mild, diffuse tenderness on deep palpation WITHOUT rebound, guarding, or rigidity; soft, bowel sounds present; no epigastric mass; no localized tenderness.
Abdomen: NO rebound tenderness, guarding, or rigidity (argues against a surgical/peritonitic abdomen).
Neuro: alert and oriented, mildly drowsy/fatigued but no focal deficit; no asterixis.

caseSummary.finalDiagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis presenting as abdominal pain
caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 24F with type 1 diabetes — recently started on an SGLT2 inhibitor and a thiazide, on levothyroxine, and using heavy over-the-counter aspirin for a sprained ankle — presents with one day of diffuse abdominal pain, vomiting, and rapid deep breathing after a flu-like illness during which she under-dosed insulin. The diagnosis is Diabetic ketoacidosis presenting as abdominal pain, reached by eliciting the focused history (including the new medications and aspirin), performing the targeted exam, and interpreting a directed workup while resolving a genuine fork against acute pancreatitis and excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (surgical abdomen, salicylate toxicity, ectopic pregnancy).
keyHistoryClues: Type 1 diabetes with insulin under-dosed during a recent viral illness
Newly started SGLT2 inhibitor (euglycemic-DKA risk) plus a thiazide; heavy OTC aspirin use
Polyuria and polydipsia preceding diffuse non-localizing pain with deep rapid breathing
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: ill-appearing, fatigued, mildly drowsy, breathing deeply and rapidly (Kussmaul respirations); fruity/acetone odor on the breath.
Skin: poor skin turgor consistent with dehydration.
Abdomen: mild, diffuse tenderness on deep palpation WITHOUT rebound, guarding, or rigidity; soft, bowel sounds present; no epigastric mass; no localized tenderness.
Abdomen: NO rebound tenderness, guarding, or rigidity (argues against a surgical/peritonitic abdomen).
Neuro: alert and oriented, mildly drowsy/fatigued but no focal deficit; no asterixis.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardia, tachypnea, volume depletion, mild drowsiness in a young type 1 diabetic on new agents).
Elicit the focused history, including the under-dosed insulin, recent illness, polyuria/polydipsia, the new SGLT2 inhibitor/thiazide, and the heavy aspirin use only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam: recognize Kussmaul breathing and dehydration and confirm the abdomen lacks peritoneal signs.

Build a differential led by Diabetic ketoacidosis with acute pancreatitis as a genuine competing driver, while naming three mechanisms of a high anion gap acidosis and excluding a surgical abdomen, salicylate toxicity, and ectopic pregnancy.

Order a focused workup (glucose/BMP, serum beta-hydroxybutyrate, VBG, lipase/triglycerides, salicylate/lactate, beta-hCG), read the patient's own EKG for hyperkalemia, and confirm the hyperglycemia + high anion gap acidosis + ketosis triad.

Commit to Diabetic ketoacidosis, resolve the fork with single discriminators, and manage with fluids first, EKG-guided potassium repletion before insulin, an insulin infusion to close the gap, stopping the SGLT2 inhibitor, and a safe-discharge plan.
commonPitfalls: Giving insulin before checking/repleting potassium despite the peaked T waves
Chasing the glucose instead of the anion gap (SGLT2-inhibitor euglycemic DKA)
Over-calling pancreatitis on a mildly elevated lipase, or salicylate toxicity on the aspirin history
Pursuing an early abdominal CT for the pain instead of checking the gap and ketones
Continuing the SGLT2 inhibitor or stopping the drip when glucose normalizes before the gap closes
managementPearls: Isotonic IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline) first
EKG-guided potassium repletion before/with insulin (hold insulin if K < 3.3)
Track the anion gap/ketones, not the glucose, and stop the SGLT2 inhibitor
The abdominal pain of DKA typically resolves as the acidosis clears
criticalWorkupItems: Glucose + BMP (anion gap, potassium, renal function)
criticalManagementItems: Check and replete potassium before/with insulin, EKG-guided (hold insulin if K < 3.3)
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.6); no acute ischemia.
Clear lungs; no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.6); no acute ischemia.
Clear lungs; no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.6); no acute ischemia.
Clear lungs; no consolidation, effusion, or free air under the diaphragm.
imageInterpretationPearls: The patient's own EKG is part of the puzzle — peaked T waves mark a serum hyperkalemia that masks total-body potassium depletion in DKA.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
